question:
Suppose that $f(x) = \sum_{i=0}^\infty c_i x^i$ is a power series for which each coefficient $c_i$ is $0$ or $1$.
Show that if $f(2/3) = 3/2$, then $f(1/2)$ must be irrational.
solution:
Suppose by way of contradiction that $f(1/2)$ is rational. Then $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} c_i 2^{-i}$ is the binary expansion of a rational number, and hence must be eventually periodic; that is, there exist some integers $m,n$ such that
$c_i = c_{m+i}$ for all $i \geq n$. We may then write
\[
f(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c_i x^i + \frac{x^n}{1-x^m} \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} c_{n+i} x^i.
\]
Evaluating at $x = 2/3$, we may equate $f(2/3) = 3/2$ with 
\[
\frac{1}{3^{n-1}} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c_i 2^i 3^{n-i-1} + \frac{2^n 3^m}{3^{n+m-1}(3^m-2^m)} \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} c_{n+i} 2^i 3^{m-1-i};
\]
since all terms on the right-hand side have odd denominator, the same must be true of the sum, a contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
Greg Marks asks whether the assumption that $f(2/3)=3/2$ further ensures that $f(1/2)$ is transcendental. 
We do not know of any existing results that would imply this. However, the following result follows from a theorem of T. Tanaka (Algebraic independence of the values of power series generated by linear recurrences, \textit{Acta Arith.} \textbf{74} (1996), 177--190), building upon work of Mahler.
Let $\{a_n\}_{n=0}^\infty$ be a linear recurrent sequence of positive integers with characteristic polynomial $P$.
Suppose that $P(0), P(1), P(-1) \neq 0$ and that no two distinct roots of $P$ have ratio which is a root of unity. Then
for $f(x) = \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^{a_n}$, the values $f(1/2)$ and $f(2/3)$ are algebraically independent over $\QQ$.
(Note that for $f$ as in the original problem, the condition on ratios of roots of $P$ fails.)

descriptive_long.question: Suppose that $function(variable) = \sum_{indexer=0}^{\infty} coefficient\, variable^{indexer}$ is a power series for which each coefficient $coefficient$ is $0$ or $1$.\nShow that if $function(2/3) = 3/2$, then $function(1/2)$ must be irrational.
descriptive_long.solution:
Suppose by way of contradiction that $function(1/2)$ is rational. Then $\sum_{indexer=0}^{\infty} coefficient 2^{-indexer}$ is the binary expansion of a rational number, and hence must be eventually periodic; that is, there exist some integers $periodlen,offset$ such that
$coefficient = c_{periodlen+indexer}$ for all $indexer \ge offset$. We may then write
\[
function(variable) = \sum_{indexer=0}^{offset-1} coefficient\, variable^{indexer} +  rac{variable^{offset}}{1-variable^{periodlen}} \sum_{indexer=0}^{periodlen-1} c_{offset+indexer}\, variable^{indexer}.
\]
Evaluating at $variable = 2/3$, we may equate $function(2/3) = 3/2$ with 
\[
 rac{1}{3^{offset-1}} \sum_{indexer=0}^{offset-1} coefficient 2^{indexer} 3^{offset-indexer-1} +  rac{2^{offset} 3^{periodlen}}{3^{offset+periodlen-1}(3^{periodlen}-2^{periodlen})} \sum_{indexer=0}^{periodlen-1} c_{offset+indexer} 2^{indexer} 3^{periodlen-1-indexer};
\]
since all terms on the right-hand side have odd denominator, the same must be true of the sum, a contradiction.


oindent
	extbf{Remark:}
Greg Marks asks whether the assumption that $function(2/3)=3/2$ further ensures that $function(1/2)$ is transcendental. 
We do not know of any existing results that would imply this. However, the following result follows from a theorem of T. Tanaka (Algebraic independence of the values of power series generated by linear recurrences, 	extit{Acta Arith.} 	extbf{74} (1996), 177--190), building upon work of Mahler.
Let $\{sequenceterm\}_{offset=0}^{\infty}$ be a linear recurrent sequence of positive integers with characteristic polynomial $polynomial$.
Suppose that $polynomial(0),\, polynomial(1),\, polynomial(-1) 
eq 0$ and that no two distinct roots of $polynomial$ have ratio which is a root of unity. Then
for $function(variable) = \sum_{offset=0}^{\infty} variable^{sequenceterm}$, the values $function(1/2)$ and $function(2/3)$ are algebraically independent over $\QQ$.
(Note that for $function$ as in the original problem, the condition on ratios of roots of $polynomial$ fails.)

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Suppose that $fjordscape(sandcastle) = \sum_{i=0}^\infty guacamole_{i} \, sandcastle^{i}$ is a power series for which each coefficient $guacamole_{i}$ is $0$ or $1$.
Show that if $fjordscape(2/3) = 3/2$, then $fjordscape(1/2)$ must be irrational.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Suppose by way of contradiction that $fjordscape(1/2)$ is rational. Then $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} guacamole_{i}\, 2^{-i}$ is the binary expansion of a rational number, and hence must be eventually periodic; that is, there exist some integers $laryngitis,chandelier$ such that
$guacamole_{i} = guacamole_{laryngitis+i}$ for all $i \geq chandelier$. We may then write
\[
fjordscape(sandcastle) = \sum_{i=0}^{chandelier-1} guacamole_{i} \, sandcastle^{i} + \frac{sandcastle^{chandelier}}{1-sandcastle^{laryngitis}} \sum_{i=0}^{laryngitis-1} guacamole_{chandelier+i} \, sandcastle^{i}.
\]
Evaluating at $sandcastle = 2/3$, we may equate $fjordscape(2/3) = 3/2$ with 
\[
\frac{1}{3^{chandelier-1}} \sum_{i=0}^{chandelier-1} guacamole_{i} 2^{i} 3^{chandelier-i-1} + \frac{2^{chandelier} 3^{laryngitis}}{3^{chandelier+laryngitis-1}(3^{laryngitis}-2^{laryngitis})} \sum_{i=0}^{laryngitis-1} guacamole_{chandelier+i} 2^{i} 3^{laryngitis-1-i};
\]
since all terms on the right-hand side have odd denominator, the same must be true of the sum, a contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
Greg Marks asks whether the assumption that $fjordscape(2/3)=3/2$ further ensures that $fjordscape(1/2)$ is transcendental. 
We do not know of any existing results that would imply this. However, the following result follows from a theorem of T. Tanaka (Algebraic independence of the values of power series generated by linear recurrences, \textit{Acta Arith.} \textbf{74} (1996), 177--190), building upon work of Mahler.
Let $\{gingerroot_{chandelier}\}_{chandelier=0}^\infty$ be a linear recurrent sequence of positive integers with characteristic polynomial $scrollwork$.
Suppose that $scrollwork(0), scrollwork(1), scrollwork(-1) \neq 0$ and that no two distinct roots of $scrollwork$ have ratio which is a root of unity. Then
for $fjordscape(sandcastle) = \sum_{chandelier=0}^\infty sandcastle^{gingerroot_{chandelier}}$, the values $fjordscape(1/2)$ and $fjordscape(2/3)$ are algebraically independent over $\QQ$.
(Note that for $fjordscape$ as in the original problem, the condition on ratios of roots of $scrollwork$ fails.)

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Suppose that $immutable(constantval) = \sum_{aggregate=0}^\infty noncoeffic constantval^{aggregate}$ is a power series for which each coefficient $noncoeffic$ is $0$ or $1$.
Show that if $immutable(2/3) = 3/2$, then $immutable(1/2)$ must be irrational.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Suppose by way of contradiction that $immutable(1/2)$ is rational. Then $\sum_{aggregate=0}^{\infty} noncoeffic 2^{-aggregate}$ is the binary expansion of a rational number, and hence must be eventually periodic; that is, there exist some integers $fractional,continuous$ such that
$noncoeffic = noncoeffic$ for all $aggregate \geq continuous$. We may then write
\[
immutable(constantval) = \sum_{aggregate=0}^{continuous-1} noncoeffic constantval^{aggregate} + \frac{constantval^{continuous}}{1-constantval^{fractional}} \sum_{aggregate=0}^{fractional-1} noncoeffic constantval^{aggregate}.
\]
Evaluating at $constantval = 2/3$, we may equate $immutable(2/3) = 3/2$ with 
\[
\frac{1}{3^{continuous-1}} \sum_{aggregate=0}^{continuous-1} noncoeffic 2^{aggregate} 3^{continuous-aggregate-1} + \frac{2^{continuous} 3^{fractional}}{3^{continuous+fractional-1}(3^{fractional}-2^{fractional})} \sum_{aggregate=0}^{fractional-1} noncoeffic 2^{aggregate} 3^{fractional-1-aggregate};
\]
since all terms on the right-hand side have odd denominator, the same must be true of the sum, a contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
Greg Marks asks whether the assumption that $immutable(2/3)=3/2$ further ensures that $immutable(1/2)$ is transcendental. 
We do not know of any existing results that would imply this. However, the following result follows from a theorem of T. Tanaka (Algebraic independence of the values of power series generated by linear recurrences, \textit{Acta Arith.} \textbf{74} (1996), 177--190), building upon work of Mahler.
Let $\{singularval\}_{continuous=0}^\infty$ be a linear recurrent sequence of positive integers with characteristic polynomial $singleton$.
Suppose that $singleton(0), singleton(1), singleton(-1) \neq 0$ and that no two distinct roots of $singleton$ have ratio which is a root of unity. Then
for $immutable(constantval) = \sum_{continuous=0}^\infty constantval^{singularval}$, the values $immutable(1/2)$ and $immutable(2/3)$ are algebraically independent over $\QQ$.
(Note that for $immutable$ as in the original problem, the condition on ratios of roots of $singleton$ fails.)

garbled_string.question:
Suppose that $zqtmpyfw(vzktdnhg) = \sum_{hsplrkbt=0}^\infty qzxwvtnp_{hsplrkbt} vzktdnhg^{hsplrkbt}$ is a power series for which each coefficient $qzxwvtnp_{hsplrkbt}$ is $0$ or $1$.
Show that if $zqtmpyfw(2/3) = 3/2$, then $zqtmpyfw(1/2)$ must be irrational.
garbled_string.solution:
Suppose by way of contradiction that $zqtmpyfw(1/2)$ is rational. Then $\sum_{hsplrkbt=0}^{\infty} qzxwvtnp_{hsplrkbt} 2^{-hsplrkbt}$ is the binary expansion of a rational number, and hence must be eventually periodic; that is, there exist some integers $hjgrksla,pkqjzrue$ such that
$qzxwvtnp_{hsplrkbt} = qzxwvtnp_{hjgrksla+hsplrkbt}$ for all $hsplrkbt \geq pkqjzrue$. We may then write
\[
zqtmpyfw(vzktdnhg) = \sum_{hsplrkbt=0}^{pkqjzrue-1} qzxwvtnp_{hsplrkbt} vzktdnhg^{hsplrkbt} + \frac{vzktdnhg^{pkqjzrue}}{1-vzktdnhg^{hjgrksla}} \sum_{hsplrkbt=0}^{hjgrksla-1} qzxwvtnp_{pkqjzrue+hsplrkbt} vzktdnhg^{hsplrkbt}.
\]
Evaluating at $vzktdnhg = 2/3$, we may equate $zqtmpyfw(2/3) = 3/2$ with 
\[
\frac{1}{3^{pkqjzrue-1}} \sum_{hsplrkbt=0}^{pkqjzrue-1} qzxwvtnp_{hsplrkbt} 2^{hsplrkbt} 3^{pkqjzrue-hsplrkbt-1} + \frac{2^{pkqjzrue} 3^{hjgrksla}}{3^{pkqjzrue+hjgrksla-1}(3^{hjgrksla}-2^{hjgrksla})} \sum_{hsplrkbt=0}^{hjgrksla-1} qzxwvtnp_{pkqjzrue+hsplrkbt} 2^{hsplrkbt} 3^{hjgrksla-1-hsplrkbt};
\]
since all terms on the right-hand side have odd denominator, the same must be true of the sum, a contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
Greg Marks asks whether the assumption that $zqtmpyfw(2/3)=3/2$ further ensures that $zqtmpyfw(1/2)$ is transcendental. 
We do not know of any existing results that would imply this. However, the following result follows from a theorem of T. Tanaka (Algebraic independence of the values of power series generated by linear recurrences, \textit{Acta Arith.} \textbf{74} (1996), 177--190), building upon work of Mahler.
Let $\{wvbsqnez_{pkqjzrue}\}_{pkqjzrue=0}^\infty$ be a linear recurrent sequence of positive integers with characteristic polynomial $rsjdvkhm$.
Suppose that $rsjdvkhm(0), rsjdvkhm(1), rsjdvkhm(-1) \neq 0$ and that no two distinct roots of $rsjdvkhm$ have ratio which is a root of unity. Then
for $zqtmpyfw(vzktdnhg) = \sum_{pkqjzrue=0}^\infty vzktdnhg^{wvbsqnez_{pkqjzrue}}$, the values $zqtmpyfw(1/2)$ and $zqtmpyfw(2/3)$ are algebraically independent over $\QQ$.
(Note that for $zqtmpyfw$ as in the original problem, the condition on ratios of roots of $rsjdvkhm$ fails.)

kernel_variant.question:
Let
\[
   f(x)=\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} c_i x^{i}, \qquad c_i\in\{0,1\},
\]
and suppose that
\[
   f\!\left(\frac45\right)=\frac52.
\]
Prove that the number \(f(1/2)\) is irrational.
kernel_variant.solution:
Proof (corrected in full detail).  Suppose, for contradiction, that

  f(1/2)=\sum_{i=0}^\infty c_i2^{-i}

is rational.  Then its binary expansion is eventually periodic, so there are integers n\ge1 and m\ge1 such that

  c_{i+m}=c_i  \quad\text{for all }i\ge n.

Accordingly, we split the power series into a finite part plus a repeating tail.  Writing

  A(x)=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}c_i x^i,
  B(x)=\sum_{i=0}^{m-1}c_{n+i}x^i,

we have for all |x|<1,

  f(x)=A(x)+x^n\sum_{i=0}^\infty c_{n+i}x^i
       =A(x)+\frac{x^n}{1-x^m}\,B(x).

Now evaluate at x=4/5.  We check that each of the two summands on the right is a rational number whose denominator (in lowest terms) is a power of 5 and hence odd.

1)  Finite part:

   A(4/5)=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}c_i\bigl(4/5\bigr)^i
          =\frac1{5^{n-1}}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}c_i\,4^i\,5^{\,n-1-i}.

   Since c_i\in\{0,1\}, the numerator is an integer and the denominator is 5^{n-1}, which is odd.

2)  Tail part:

   T:=\frac{(4/5)^n}{1-(4/5)^m}\sum_{i=0}^{m-1}c_{n+i}(4/5)^i.

   First,
     (4/5)^n=4^n/5^n,
     1-(4/5)^m=(5^m-4^m)/5^m,
   and
     \sum_{i=0}^{m-1}c_{n+i}(4/5)^i
       =\frac1{5^{m-1}}\sum_{i=0}^{m-1}c_{n+i}\,4^i\,5^{\,m-1-i}.

   Therefore

   T=\frac{4^n}{5^n}\cdot\frac{5^m}{5^m-4^m}\cdot\frac1{5^{m-1}}\sum_{i=0}^{m-1}c_{n+i}\,4^i\,5^{\,m-1-i}
     =\frac{\displaystyle4^n\sum_{i=0}^{m-1}c_{n+i}4^i5^{\,m-1-i}\,5^m}{5^{n+(m-1)}\,(5^m-4^m)}
     =\frac{\text{integer}}{5^{n+m-1}(5^m-4^m)}.

   Here 5^{n+m-1} is odd and (5^m-4^m)=\text{odd}-\text{even}=\text{odd}.  Thus T in lowest terms also has an odd denominator.

Adding, f(4/5)=A(4/5)+T is a rational number whose denominator (in lowest terms) is odd.  But by hypothesis

  f(4/5)=5/2

whose denominator 2 is even.  This contradiction shows that f(1/2) cannot be rational.  Hence f(1/2) is irrational, as claimed.  \boxed{}

core_steps: Assume f(1/2) is rational, so the binary (base-2) expansion is eventually periodic.
Translate “eventually periodic” into c_{n+i}=c_i for i≥n, giving a finite part plus a geometric-series tail.
Evaluate this at x=2/3; every denominator that appears is a product of powers of 3 and (3^m−2^m), hence odd.
But f(2/3)=3/2 has an even denominator, contradiction ⇒ f(1/2) irrational.

slot1.description: Rational value assigned to f at the special point; only the property “irreducible denominator even” is used.

slot2.description: Special evaluation point a/b with a even, b odd (here 2/3); guarantees all constructed denominators stay odd.
